content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 20, 2016

Law Offices of
Andreas C. Rockas
1121 L Street, Suite 103
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-16-017

Dear Mr. Rockas:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the Conflict of Interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Government Code Section 1090. Please note that we
do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of
interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

     We are required to forward your request for advice under Section 1090 and all pertinent
facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Sacramento County District
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written
response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against
any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

     May an ALPHA Executive participate in merger negotiations between ALPHA and BETA,
two Joint Powers Authorities, when a merger would result in the ALPHA executive's job being
eliminated?

CONCLUSION

     Yes an ALPHA Executive may participate in merger negotiations under the rule of necessity
because no other person is qualified to represent ALPHA in such negotiations.


___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-017
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the attorney representing the Association of California Healthcare Districts
("ACHD"). ACHD is seeking guidance as to how to deal with the employment agreements of
executives of a joint powers authority interested in merging with another joint powers authority
without violating the Act or Government Code Section 1090.

ACHD is a California nonprofit mutual benefit corporation incorporated on May 29, 1951.
ACHD formed ALPHA Fund ("ALPHA") in 1976, a joint powers authority. ACHD also formed
BETA Healthcare Group Risk Management Authority ("BETA") in 1979, also a joint powers
authority.

ALPHA is self-insurance worker's compensation n program. ALPHA assists its members
with accumulating, administering and investing funds for self-insuring their collective worker's
compensation liability. ALPHA's membership consists of healthcare districts, district hospitals, and
nonprofit healthcare organizations that provide hospitals, clinics, hospice, or other services similar
to those provided by healthcare districts. ACHD serves as ALPHA's "coordinator" and ALPHA's
board is ACHD's "Governing Board." ALPHA runs a sophisticated enterprise with gross receipts of
over $45 million per year, over $111 million in revenues, and 52 full time employees.

BETA is a self-insurance program, much like ALPHA. BETA pools and administers its
members' funds for the purpose of self-insuring as a group healthcare entity comprehensive liability
coverage, directors' and officers' liability coverage, automobile liability and physical damage
coverage, and medical group professional liability coverage to nonprofit health care providers and
healthcare districts. BETA is currently prohibited from offering workers' compensation coverage to
its members pursuant to its termination agreement with ACHD. ACHD's control over BETA ended
in October 1989.

ACHD would like to explore the opportunity of merging ALPHA and BETA. If a merger
were to happen, BETA would likely be the surviving entity. Pursuant to employment agreements
with its executives on July 2, 2012. ALPHA is responsible for 88% to 91% of the ALPHA
executives' compensation and ACHD is responsible for the remainder. Pursuant to the employment
agreements between ACHD and the ALPHA executives, the executives are to hold their positions
and if they are terminated without cause they are entitled to one year's severance pay. These
agreements were completely separate from and occurred long before any contemplated merger
between ALPHA and BETA. If ALPHA and BETA were to merge, the ALPHA executives'
positions would be eliminated as there are no equivalent positions available at BETA. The parties
have had no discussions about BETA employing any of the ALPHA Executives. It is possible that
BETA could hire the ALPHA Executives into new roles.

Though there have been preliminary discussions of a merger between the parties, ALPHA
executives have not been involved in any discussions about their employment or how a merger
would affect their positions. No merger agreement has yet been entered into and all preliminary
discussions have been terminated for the time being.

File No. A-16-017
Page No. 3

The Executive Director of ACHD, Ken Cohen, has been handling all discussions regarding
the ALPHA executives' employment agreements. Mr. Cohen is not an employee of either ALPHA
or BETA and his employment status would be unaffected by a merger of these two JPAs. The
discussions he participated in involved analyzing whether it is better to pay the ALPHA executives
a severance and let them go or keep them on and have ACHD cover the 88% to 91% of the
executives' pay it currently covers and have BETA assume the remainder. The ALPHA Executives'
employment would not be addressed in the merger documents.

Should merger negotiations occur, it would be impossible for ALPHA to participate in the
merger discussions without involvement of at least one of the ALPHA Executives, the Chief
Executive Officer. It would be impossible because no one at ALPHA or ACHD has the breadth of
knowledge of ALPHA's business modules to create a sound negotiating strategy or properly answer
questions from BETA. The merger documents will contemplate how ALPHA and BETA's business
lines integrate, how ALPHA employees (not ALPHA Executives) should be treated, and what terms
or obligations apply pre-closing and post-closing to ALPHA's line of business. ACHD does not
handle the day to day operations of ALPHA and none of ACHD's employees including its senior
officer, Ken Cohen, have enough experience with workers' compensation programs generally or
ALPHA specifically to lead the negotiations. Additionally, ACHD would be a party to the
negotiations in its own right and Ken Cohen has been tasked with representing ACHD's interests. It
would be challenging for him to adequately represent ACHD's interests and ALPHA Fund's
interests in the same negotiations.

The ACHD Board of Directors would like to know if the ALPHA Executives can participate
in the merger negotiations so long as their employment status is not discussed during those
negotiations. Once the details of the merger are complete, each ALPHA Executive could negotiate
his employment in his personal capacity. The employment status of these executives would
eventually become part of the merger agreement as well.

ANALYSIS

1) May the ALPHA Executives participate in the merger negotiations?

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended not only to strike at
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety. (City of Imperial Beach v.
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a
contract in which the official has a financial interest. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289,
333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.)
The prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all
parties. (Id. at pp. 646-649.)

We employ the following six-step analysis to determine whether the ALPHA Executives
will have a conflict of interest under Section 1090.

File No. A-16-017
Page No. 4

Step One: Will the provisions of Section 1090 apply to the ALPHA Executives?

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by anybody or board of which they are members." As employees of ALPHA (a governmental entity), the ALPHA Executives are subject to the provisions of Section 1090.

Step Two: Does the decision at issue involve a contract?

To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 (1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and 1097 require that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 'contract.'" (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.)

In this case the merger between ALPHA and BETA will result in a contract between those entities.

Step Three: Will the public employee or official be making or participating in making a contract?

Participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae, (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall v. City of Taft, (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.)

When an employee, rather than a board member, is financially interested in a contract, the employee's agency is prohibited from making the contract only if the employee was involved in the contract-making process. Therefore, as long as the employee plays no role whatsoever in the contracting process (either because such participation is outside the scope of the employee's duties or because the employee disqualifies himself or herself from all such participation), the employee's agency is not prohibited from contracting with the employee or the business entity in which the employee is interested. (See 80 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 41 (1997) [firefighters permitted to sell a product, which they invented in their private capacity, to their fire department so long as they did not participate in the sale in their official capacity]; 63 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 868 (1980) [real estate tax appraiser could purchase property within the county at a tax-deeded land sale where he did not participate in or influence the appraisal].

Typically, a contract is "made" on mutual assent of the involved parties. (Stigall, supra, at p. 569.) In addition, making or participating in making a contract has been broadly construed to include those instances where a public official has influence over the contract or its terms. (See 80 Ops.Cal. Atty. Gen. 41.) In this case, if the ALPHA executives are involved in the merger negotiations, they will be influencing the contract terms and therefore they will be making or participating in the contract.

File No. A-16-017
Page No. 5

Step Four: Does the official have a financial interest in the contract?

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest" (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333), and officials are deemed to have a financial
interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) Although Section 1090 nowhere
specifically defines the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General Opinions state that
prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, as well as
the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (See e.g., Thomson, supra, at pp.
645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5.)

The ALPHA Executives' employment will be affected as a result of any merger between
ALPHA and BETA. While it is currently unclear how their employment will be affected, you have
stated that their current positions are not available at BETA. Currently, the Executives will lose their
jobs with ALPHA and be entitled to be paid one year's severance upon their departure. There is
also a possibility they will be offered new positions with BETA.

Step Five: Does either a remote interest or non-interest exception apply?

There are no applicable remote interests or non-interests in this case.

Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply?

In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a
contract that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (Dietrick Advice Letter, No. A-15-174; 88
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 110 (2005).) The rule of necessity has two facets: in procurement
situations, it has permitted a government agency to acquire an essential supply or service despite a
conflict of interest; in nonprocurement situations, it has permitted a public officer to carry out the
essential duties of the office despite a conflict of interest where the officer is the only one who may
legally act. (65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 310 (1982).) In nonprocurement situations, the rule of
necessity ensures that essential government functions are performed even where a conflict of
interest exists. (Ibid.)

Thus, to determine if the rule of necessity applies, we must examine whether negotiating
and entering into a merger agreement is an essential duty of ALPHA and whether ALPHA is the
only government entity legally capable of making the agreement.

ALPHA runs a sophisticated enterprise with gross receipts of over $45 million per year,
over $11 million in revenues, and 52 full time employees. You state it would be impossible for
ALPHA to negotiate a merger without at least one of the ALPHA Executives, the Chief Executive
Officer, but it would be possible for the ALPHA Executives to refrain from discussing anything
related to their employment agreements during the merger negotiations.

No one at ALPHA Fund or ACHD has the breadth of knowledge of ALPHA's business
modules to create a sound negotiating strategy or properly answer questions from BETA. The
merger documents will contemplate how ALPHA and BETA's business lines integrate, how
ALPHA employees (not ALPHA Executives) should be treated, and what terms or obligations
apply pre-closing and post-closing to ALPHA's line of business. ACHD does not handle the day to
day of ALPHA's line of business. ACHD does not handle the day to

File No. A-16-017
Page No. 6

day operations of ALPHA and none of ACHD's employees including its senior officer, Ken Cohen,
have enough experience with workers' compensation programs generally or ALPHA specifically to
lead the negotiations. Additionally, ACHD would be a party to the negotiations and Ken Cohen has
been tasked with representing ACHD's interests. It would be challenging for him to adequately
represent ACHD's interests and ALPHA Fund's interests in the same negotiations.

There is simply no interest served by prohibiting the necessary ALPHA Executive from
participating in the merger agreement because here the employment agreements that would be
affected are not to be contemplated with the merger agreement as they will be handled at a later
date and there is no other qualified person to handle the merger discussions. It would be impractical
to require an entity to enter into a merger agreement without representing itself. Accordingly, we
find that the rule of necessity applies to the potential merger contract issue.

Based on the facts presented, we conclude that Section 1090 applies to any potential
contract between ACHD, ALPHA and BETA. However, under the rule of necessity, an ALPHA
Chief Executive Officer may participate in the merger negotiations and enter into one or more of
these potential contracts on behalf of ALPHA.

Political Reform Act

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions provide that no public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)

Applying the Act's conflict of interest rules, we consider whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the ALPHA Executives' participation in the merger agreement would result in a
material financial effect on their financial interests.

Under the Act, the ALPHA Executives have a financial interest in their sources of income.
In this case both ACHD and ALPHA pay the salaries of the ALPHA Executives. Under the Act,
income does not include salary received from a state, local or federal government agency. (Section
82030(b)(2).) Therefore, income from ALPHA to its executives does not qualify as a financial
interest under the Act because ALPHA is a government entity. However, as noted above the
ALPHA Executives also receive part (88%-91%) of their salary from ACHD which is likely over
$500 per year. ACHD is not a government entity, therefore they would have a financial interest in
ACHD. (Section 87103.)

Regulation 18701 provides guidance on determining whether a financial effect is reasonably
foreseeable:

"A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if
the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision
before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a
proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, contract, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial
interest . . ."

File No. A-16-017
Page No. 7

In this case, ALPHA and ACHD are the subject of and parties in the governmental decisions
before the Executives and therefore the financial effect on their financial interest is presumed to be
reasonable foreseeable.

Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) further provides that an effect on a public official's financial
interest in a source of income is material when:

". . . The source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is otherwise
named or identified as the subject of the proceeding . . .."

Here both ALPHA and ACHD are contracting parties, therefore there would be a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon the ALPHA Executive's' financial interests and they
would have a conflict under the Act by participating in the merger agreements.

Once it has been determined that there would be a conflict we next look to examine whether
any exceptions apply.

Regulation 18705 provides us with the "Legally Required Participation" exception: "A
public official who has a financial interest in a decision may establish that he or she is legally
required to make or to participate in the making of a governmental decision within the meaning of
Section 87101 only if there exists no alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and
terms of the statute authorizing the decision."

As stated above, ALPHA runs a sophisticated enterprise with gross receipts of over $45
million per year, over $111 million in revenues and 52 full time employees. No one at ALPHA
Fund or ACHD has the breadth of knowledge of ALPHA's business modules to create a sound
negotiating strategy or properly answer questions from BETA. The merger documents will
contemplate how ALPHA and BETA's business lines integrate, how ALPHA employees (not
ALPHA Executives) should be treated, and what terms or obligations apply pre-closing and post-
closing to ALPHA's line of business. ACHD does not handle the day to day operations of ALPHA
and none of ACHD's employees including its senior officer, Ken Cohen, have enough experience
with workers' compensation programs generally or ALPHA specifically to lead the negotiations.
Additionally, ACHD would be a party to the negotiations and Ken Cohen has been tasked with
representing ACHD's interests. It would be challenging for Mr. Cohen to adequately represent
ACHD's interests and ALPHA Fund's interests in the same negotiations.

It would be impractical to require an entity to enter into a merger agreement without
representing itself. In this case there is no alternative source of decision consistent with the
purposes and terms of general merger and contracting procedures.

An official who participates as a result of invoking the legally required participation
exception must announce the conflict with particularity. There are different methods for announcing
the conflict depending on whether the decision is made at an open meeting, a closed session of an
open meeting, or neither. If the decision is made at an open meeting the conflict must be announced
publicly at the meeting, if in closed session then announced before or after the closed session at the

File No. A-16-017
Page No. 8

public portion of the meeting, and if the decisions are made elsewhere then the announcement must
be made in writing as described in Regulation 18705 (copy enclosed).

Also, only participation by the smallest number of officials with a conflict that are "legally
required" in order for the decision to be made is allowed. A random means of selection may be used
to select only the number of officials needed, however, in this case it has been determined that only
ALPHA's Chief Executive Officer is needed. When an official is selected, he or she is selected for
the duration of the proceedings in all related matters until his or her participation is no longer
legally required, or the need for invoking the exception no longer exists. (Regulation 18705.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

sections.question:
May an ALPHA Executive participate in merger negotiations between ALPHA and BET A, 
two Joint Powers Authorities, when a merger would result in the ALPHA executive's job being 
eliminated?
sections.conclusion:
Yes an ALPHA Executive may participate in merger negotiations under the rule of necessity 
because no other person is qualified to represent ALPHA in such negotiations.

from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section l 090 is concerned with

FileNo. A-16-017

•contract."' (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.)

entities. 
Step Three: \Viii the public employee or official be making or participating in making 
a contract? 
Participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving preliminary

and solicitation for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae, (1968) 262

569.) In addition, making or participating in making a contract has been broadly construed to

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has

The ALPHA Executives' employment will be affected as a result of any merger between 
ALPHA and BET A. While it is currently unclear how their employment will be affected, you have

Step Six: Docs the rule of necessity apply? 
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a 
contract that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (Dietrick Advice Letter, No. A-15-174; 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. l 06, 110 (2005).) The rule of necessity has two facets: in procurement 
situations, it has pennitted a government agency to acquire an essential supply or service despite a 
conflict of interest; in nonprocurement situations, it has pennitted a public officer to carry out the 
essential duties of the office despite a conflict of interest where the officer is the only one who may 
legally act. (65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 310 (1982).) In nonprocurement situations, the rule of 
necessity ensures that essential government functions are performed even where a conflict of 
interest exists. (Ibid.) 
Thus, to determine if the rule of necessity applies, we must examine whether negotiating 
and entering into a merger agreement is an essential duty of ALPHA and whether ALPHA is the 
only government entity legally capable of making the agreement. 
ALPHA runs a sophisticated enterprise with gross receipts of over $45 million per year, 
over S 111 million in revenues, and 52 full time employees. You state it would be impossible for 
ALPHA to negotiate a merger without at ]east one of the ALPHA Executives, the Chief Executive 
Officer, but it would be possible for the ALPHA Executives to refrain from discussing anything 
related to their employment agreements during the merger negotiations. 
No one at ALPHA Fund or ACHD has the breadth of knowledge of ALPHA•s business 
modules to create a sound negotiating strategy or properly answer questions from BET A. The 
merger documents will contemplate how ALPHA and BET A's business lines integrate, how 
ALPHA employees (not ALPHA Executives) should be treated, and what terms or obligations 
apply pre-closing and post-closing to ALPHA's line of business. ACHD does not handle the day to 


File No. A-16-01 7  
day operations of ALPHA and none of ACHD's employees including its senior officer, Ken Cohen, 
have enough experience with workers' compensation programs generally or ALPHA specifically to 
lead the negotiations. Additionally, ACHD would be a party to the negotiations and Ken Cohen has 
been tasked with representing ACHD's interests. It would be challenging for him to adequately 
represent ACHD's interests and ALPHA Fund's interests in the same negotiations. 
There is simply no interest served by prohibiting the necessary ALPHA Executive from 
participating in the merger agreement because here the employment agreements that would be 
affected are not to be contemplated with the merger agreement as they will be handled at a later 
date and there is no other qualified person to handle the merger discussions. It would be impractical 
to require an entity to enter into a merger agreement without representing itself. Accordingly, we 
find that the rule of necessity applies to the potential merger contract at issue. 
Based on the facts presented, we conclude that Section 1090 applies to any potential 
contract between ACHD, ALPHA and BETA. However, under the rule of necessity, an ALPHA 
Chief Executive Officer may participate in the merger negotiations and enter into one or more of 
these potential contracts on behalf of ALPHA. 
Political Reform Act 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions provide that no public official at any level of state 
or local government shall make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his or her 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to 
know he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) 
Applying the Act's conflict of interest rules, we consider whether it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the ALPHA Executives' participation in the merger agreement would result in a 
material financial effect on their financial interests. 
Under the Act, the ALPHA Executives have a financial interest in their sources of income. 
In this case both ACHD and ALPHA pay the salaries of the ALPHA Executives. Under the Act, 
income does not include salary received from a state, local or federal government agency. (Section 
82030(b)(2).) Therefore, income from ALPHA to its executives does not qualify as a financial 
interest under the Act because ALPHA is a government entity. However, as noted above the 
ALPHA Executives also receive part (88%-91 %) of their salary from ACHD which is likely over 
$500 per year. ACHD is not a government entity, therefore they would have a financial interest in 
ACHD. (Section 87103.) 
Regulation 18701 provides guidance on determining whether a financial effect is reasonably 
foreseeable: 
"A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if 
the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision 
before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a 
proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or 
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial 
interest . . .. "  
In this case, ALPHA and ACHD are the subject of and parties in the governmental decisions 
before the Executives and therefore the financial effect on their financial interest is presumed to be 
reasonable foreseeable. 
Regulation J8702.3(a) (1) further provides that an effect on a public official's financial 
interest in a source of income is material when: 
" . . .  The source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is otherwise 
named or identified as the subject of the proceeding . . .. " 
Here both ALPHA and ACHD are contracting parties, therefore there would be a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect upon the ALPHA Executive's' financial interests and they 
would have a conflict under the Act by participating in the merger agreements. 
Once it has been detennined that there would be a conflict we next look to examine whether 
any exceptions apply. 
Regulation 18705 provides us with the "Legally Required Participation" exception: "A 
public official who has a financial interest in a decision may establish that he or she is legally 
required to make or to participate in the making of a governmental decision within the meaning of 
Section 87101 only ifthere exists no alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and 
terms of the statute authorizing the decision." 
As stated above, ALPHA runs a sophisticated enterprise with gross receipts of over S45 
million per year, over S 1 1 1  million in revenues, and 52 full time employees. No one at ALPHA 
Fund or ACHD has the breadth of knowledge of ALPHA's business modules to create a sound 
negotiating strategy or properly answer questions from BET A. The merger documents will 
contemplate how ALPHA and BETA's business lines integrate, how ALPHA employees (not 
ALPHA Executives) should be treated, and what terms or obligations apply pre-closing and post­
closing to ALPHA's line of business. ACHD does not handle the day to day operations of ALPHA 
and none of ACHD's employees including its senior officer, Ken Cohen, have enough experience 
with workers' compensation programs generally or ALPHA specifically to lead the negotiations. 
Additionally, ACHD would be a party to the negotiations and Ken Cohen has been tasked with 
representing ACHD's interests. It would be challenging for Mr. Cohen to adequately represent 
ACHD's interests and ALPHA Fund's interests in the same negotiations. 
It would be impractical to require an entity to enter into a merger agreement without 
representing itself. In this case there is no alternative source of decision consistent with the 
purposes and tenns of general merger and contracting procedures. 
An official who participates as a result of invoking the legally required participation 
exception must announce the conflict with particularity. There are different methods for announcing 
the conflict depending on whether the decision is made at an open meeting, a closed session of an 
open meeting, or neither. If the decision is made at an open meeting the conflict must be announced 
publicly at the meeting, if in closed session then announced before or after the closed session at the  
public portion of the meeting, and if the decisions are made elsewhere then the announcement must 
be made in writing as described in Regulation 18705 ( copy enclosed). 
Also, only participation by the smallest number of officials with a conflict that are "legally 
required" in order for the decision to be made is allowed. A random means of selection may be used 
to select only the number of officials needed, however, in this case it has been determined that only 
ALPHA's Chief Executive Officer is needed. When an official is selected, he or she is selected for 
the duration of the proceedings in all related matters until his or her participation is no longer 
legally required, or the need for invoking the exception no longer exists. (Regulation 1 8705.)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May an ALPHA Executive participate in merger negotiations between ALPHA and BET A, 
two Joint Powers Authorities, when a merger would result in the ALPHA executive's job being 
eliminated?

CONCLUSION: Yes an ALPHA Executive may participate in merger negotiations under the rule of necessity 
because no other person is qualified to represent ALPHA in such negotiations.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 April 20, 2016 Law Offices of Andreas C. Rockas 1121 L Street, Suite 103 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-017 Dear Mr. Rockas: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the Conflict of Interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Government Code Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. We are required to forward your request for advice under Section 1090 and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Sacramento County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTION May an ALPHA Executive participate in merger negotiations between ALPHA and BETA, two Joint Powers Authorities, when a merger would result in the ALPHA executive's job being eliminated? CONCLUSION Yes an ALPHA Executive may participate in merger negotiations under the rule of necessity because no other person is qualified to represent ALPHA in such negotiations. ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-017 Page No. 2 FACTS You are the attorney representing the Association of California Healthcare Districts ("ACHD"). ACHD is seeking guidance as to how to deal with the employment agreements of executives of a joint powers authority interested in merging with another joint powers authority without violating the Act or Government Code Section 1090. ACHD is a California nonprofit mutual benefit corporation incorporated on May 29, 1951. ACHD formed ALPHA Fund ("ALPHA") in 1976, a joint powers authority. ACHD also formed BETA Healthcare Group Risk Management Authority ("BETA") in 1979, also a joint powers authority. ALPHA is self-insurance worker's compensation n program. ALPHA assists its members with accumulating, administering and investing funds for self-insuring their collective worker's compensation liability. ALPHA's membership consists of healthcare districts, district hospitals, and nonprofit healthcare organizations that provide hospitals, clinics, hospice, or other services similar to those provided by healthcare districts. ACHD serves as ALPHA's "coordinator" and ALPHA's board is ACHD's "Governing Board." ALPHA
